entries.title: What IDOR is
entries.body:
IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) - the application grants direct access to an object (a file, a DB record, a URL) based on user input without checking permissions. You change the identifier in the URL or API request -> you read/modify/delete someone else's data.

Example: a profile is available at https://example.com/profile?user_id=123. If the server does not check that the current user is allowed to view profile 123, it is enough to substitute someone else's id:
https://example.com/profile?user_id=124

entries.title: IDOR diagram - requesting someone else's document

meta.caption: A user requests their own document #1000 - the server serves it. Then they request document #1002 (someone else's) - and the server serves it anyway, without checking the owner. This is exactly IDOR. Source: PayloadsAllTheThings.

entries.title: Where to look for and how to test IDOR
entries.body:
• Any identifier parameter: user_id, account, order, doc, file, invoice, message, id in the URL, in the query, in the body, in JSON, in headers, in cookies.
• Create two accounts (A and B). Perform the action as A, substitute the identifier of B's object -> if access is granted, it is IDOR.
• Check not only reads (GET) but also modification/deletion (POST/PUT/DELETE).
• The Burp extensions Authz / AuthMatrix / Autorize often help - they automatically replay requests from another session and highlight the difference.

entries.title: Enumerating numeric IDs (increment/decrement)
entries.body:
Increment/decrement the value to reach someone else's data.

• Decimal:    287789, 287790, 287791, ...
• Hexadecimal: 0x4642d, 0x4642e, 0x4642f, ...
• Unix epoch timestamp: 1695574808, 1695575098, ...

entries.title: Names, email, base64
entries.body:
Some identifiers are guessable (names and emails) and may open access to customer data.

• Name:  john, doe, john.doe, ...
• Email: john.doe@mail.com
• Base64 encoded value: am9obi5kb2VAbWFpbC5jb20=

entries.title: Predictable UUID/GUID v1 and MongoDB ObjectId
entries.body:
• UUID/GUID v1 is predictable if you know the creation time: 95f6e264-bb00-11ec-8833-00155d01ef00
• MongoDB ObjectId is generated predictably: 5ae9b90a2c144b9def01ec37
    - 4 bytes - seconds since the Unix epoch
    - 3 bytes - machine identifier
    - 2 bytes - process id
    - 3 bytes - a counter, starting from a random value

entries.title: ID/token as a hash of username/email
entries.body:
Sometimes an id or token is a hash of a known value, for example sha1(username), md5(email). Compute the hash yourself and substitute it.

• MD5:  098f6bcd4621d373cade4e832627b4f6
• SHA1: a94a8fe5ccb19ba61c4c0873d391e987982fbbd3
• SHA2: 9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08

entries.title: Substituting a wildcard instead of an ID
entries.body:
GET /api/users/* HTTP/1.1
GET /api/users/% HTTP/1.1
GET /api/users/_ HTTP/1.1
GET /api/users/. HTTP/1.1

entries.title: The wildcard idea
entries.body: Send a wildcard (*, %, ., _) instead of a specific ID - some backends will respond with the data of all users.

entries.title: IDOR Tips - changing the method, the type, an array, parameter pollution
entries.body:
• Change the HTTP method:    POST -> PUT
• Change the content type:   XML -> JSON
• Wrap the number in an array: {"id":19} -> {"id":[19]}
• Parameter Pollution:       user_id=hacker_id&user_id=victim_id

entries.title: Burp extensions for access checks
entries.body:
• Authz - replays requests from another session and compares the responses.
• AuthMatrix - a matrix of roles/endpoints for authorization testing.
• Autorize - automatically sends every request from a low-priv session and flags bypassed authorization.
All three are from the PortSwigger BApp Store.
